ananubodhā.meaning: through not realising, from not perceiving or understanding
ananubodhā.gram: masculine noun (from na anubujjhati), ablative singular

ananugiddho.meaning: not greedy, free of craving after
ananugiddho.gram: past participle of na anugijjhati, masculine nominative singular
breakdown: Root "to be greedy" + past participle suffix -ta (giving -giddha).

ananuppadiyamāne.meaning: (when wealth is) not being given out, not being handed over
ananuppadiyamāne.gram: present participle (passive) of na anuppadīyati, locative singular

ananuvajjā.meaning: not to be blamed, not blameworthy
ananuvajjā.gram: future passive participle of na anuvadati
breakdown: Root "to speak"; anuvadati = "to blame, censure" + participle suffix -ya.

anapāyinī.meaning: not going away, never departing (e.g. of a shadow that never leaves)

anapūjanāsu.meaning: in the gain, honour, respect, reverence, esteem and veneration of others
anapūjanāsu.note: uncertain: the headword is the truncated tail of the longer compound paralābhasakkāragarukāramānanavandanapūjanāsu, as the lexicon's own breakdown shows.

anariyo.meaning: ignoble, not noble; not spiritually healthy
breakdown: Noble; in the suttas, one advanced on the path.

anaticārinī.meaning: not transgressing; (of a wife) not unfaithful, not committing adultery

breakdown: Arrogance, conceit, high opinion of oneself (ati + √man).

anatthakāmā.meaning: wishing misfortune; desiring what is not for one's welfare

anatthapadasaṁhitā.meaning: connected with meaningless words; full of unprofitable talk

anatthasañhitaṁ.meaning: not connected with the goal; unprofitable, without benefit

anatthasaṁhito.meaning: not connected to the goal; unprofitable

anatthaviññāpakakāyavacīpayogasamuṭṭhāpi.meaning: occasioning the use of body and speech to communicate what is meaningless or harmful
anatthaviññāpakakāyavacīpayogasamuṭṭhāpi.summary: A long analytic compound from the commentarial definition of useless speech.

anatthuppādanaṁ.meaning: producing misfortune; causing what is not beneficial

anattā.meaning: non-self; the absence of an enduring self in any phenomenon
anattā.summary: The third of the three basic characteristics of existence, alongside anicca (impermanence) and dukkha (unsatisfactoriness).
anattā.goenka: Anattā, non-self, is the third of the three basic characteristics taught in the tradition: impermanence (anicca), unsatisfactoriness (dukkha) and non-self (anattā). The Anattalakkhaṇa-sutta (the Characteristics of Not Self) is dedicated to this topic. The meditator is led to recognise the impermanent nature, the suffering and the non-self character in the five aggregates of attachment (pañca upādānakkhandhā — matter, sensations, perception, mental conditioned reaction and consciousness): nowhere in this changing flow is an enduring self to be found. The term is broader than "no soul" and is not the ethical "selflessness" or the popular psychological "egolessness".

anatīto.meaning: not gone beyond, not having escaped or overcome (e.g. not exempt from old age and death)
anatīto.gram: past participle of na acceti (ati + √i), masculine nominative singular
breakdown: Gone past, escaped, overcome (past participle of acceti).

anavajjasukhaṁ.gram: neuter abstract compound noun, nominative/accusative singular

anavamānanāya.meaning: without contempt, with no disregard or disrespect

anayabyasanaṁ.meaning: distress and misfortune; calamity and disaster

anaṅgaṇassa.meaning: of one free from stain or blemish

andhakāratimisā.meaning: the gloom of blinding darkness; deep, dark blackness

anekabyādhiparipīḷitaṁ.meaning: oppressed by many diseases; vexed by manifold illness
breakdown: Oppressed, vexed (past participle of paripīḷeti, pari + √pīḷ).

anekaguṇapaṭilābhā-.meaning: the acquisition of many qualities (first part of the compound anekaguṇapaṭilābhānisaṁsaṁ, "the benefit of acquiring many qualities")
anekaguṇapaṭilābhā-.note: uncertain: this key is a hyphenated line-break fragment of anekaguṇapaṭilābhānisaṁsaṁ; see that entry.

anekaguṇapaṭilābhānisaṁsaṁ.meaning: having the benefit of acquiring many qualities

anekapariyāyena.meaning: in many ways, by many methods
breakdown: Negative prefix "not" (before a vowel): aneka = "not one, many".

anekappakāro.meaning: of many kinds; with many ramparts
breakdown: Kind, sort; rampart. (The doubled pp is sandhi doubling at the compound seam.)
anekappakāro.note: divergence: the lexicon glosses this occurrence as "many + rampart" (describing a fortified city); the same compound elsewhere means "manifold, of many kinds".

anekavihitaṁ.meaning: manifold, of many sorts; equipped in many ways
breakdown: Furnished, arranged, equipped (past participle of vidahati).

anenāti.meaning: "through this, by this" (with quotation marker ti)
anenāti.gram: pronoun ima/ena, instrumental singular + quotation particle ti
breakdown: By this (instrumental singular of the pronoun ima).
Quotation marker (iti), fused by sandhi to -nāti.

aneḷamūgatā.meaning: the state of being neither deaf nor dumb; unimpaired faculties of hearing and speech

anicca.meaning: impermanence; the arising and passing away of all conditioned phenomena
anicca.summary: The first of the three basic characteristics of existence, alongside dukkha (unsatisfactoriness) and anattā (non-self).
anicca.goenka: Anicca, impermanence, is the first of the three basic characteristics: impermanence (anicca), unsatisfactoriness (dukkha) and non-self (anattā). In the tradition it is not an abstract doctrine of change but a truth to be experienced directly: Klaus speaks of "understanding anicca (passato udayabbayaṁ)" — literally seeing the arising-and-passing — and notes that the experience of the dissolution of the body (bhaṅgañāṇa) gives a vastly more impressionable understanding of impermanence than intellectual insight alone. The canonical verse of the Hirīsutta declares: anicca vata saṅkhārā... uppādavayadhammino — conditioned things are impermanent indeed, by their nature constantly arising and vanishing. The perception of impermanence (aniccasaññā) is one of the ten saññā meditation objects. Renderings such as mere "change" or "transient" lose this experiential dimension.

anicchā.meaning: absence of desire; without lust or covetousness

anicchāsaññā.meaning: the perception of non-desire; dispassion, not desiring

anikkasāvo.meaning: not free from impurities, still stained (by the defilements)
breakdown: Free from stain or impurity (nir + kasāva "stain, impurity").

anikkhittadhuro.meaning: not laying down the burden; unflagging in responsibility
breakdown: Put down, laid aside (past participle of nikkhipati).

anindārosaṁ.meaning: without blame and without anger; free of rebuke and wrath
breakdown: Negative prefix "not" (repeated for the second member).

anivattipaṭipadaṁ.meaning: the practice of not turning back; walking the path without stopping
breakdown: Path, way of practice, walking the path.

aniṭṭhā.gram: past participle of na icchati (√is), plural or feminine form
breakdown: Wished for, pleasant, agreeable (past participle of icchati).

aniṭṭhāya.gram: past participle of na icchati, feminine oblique singular
breakdown: Wished for, welcome (past participle of icchati).
